refined_fr.refined_fr: FR-NGHE-004.1: Maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a numerical availability target, and (2) doing so within a defined measurement window. Splitting these into atomic FRs allows independent design decisions and verification strategies. The refined FR focuses on the primary functional obligation—sustaining availability performance—while treating the measurement period as a constraint context rather than a functional requirement. This formulation is solution-neutral: it does not prescribe redundancy, predictive maintenance, or any specific DP; it only states the availability outcome required.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (total time − unplanned downtime) / total time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold at the end of the evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: Maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: Establish and communicate the availability measurement methodology and evaluation period to all stakeholders.
FR-NGHE-004.3: Provide visibility into availability performance (uptime/downtime events) to Site Manager and Project Manager during the evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-004.1a: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity during component degradation or failure events.

DP-NGHE-004.1b: Design preventive maintenance scheduling and condition-monitoring protocols that detect and replace wear-prone components before failure thresholds are reached, reducing unplanned downtime.

DP-NGHE-004.1c: Establish rapid fault detection and isolation mechanisms (diagnostics, self-test routines) coupled with standardized repair procedures and spare-parts availability to minimize mean-time-to-recovery (MTTR).
DP-NGHE-004.1d: Architect modular, hot-swappable subsystem design with standardized interfaces to enable in-field replacement without full system shutdown, reducing downtime per failure event.

DP-NGHE-004.1e: Implement graceful degradation logic that allows the system to operate at reduced capacity (e.g., lower throughput, limited functionality) rather than total failure, preserving partial availability during component faults.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-NGHE-004.1: Achieve target availability through a composite strategy combining (1) redundancy and failover for critical subsystems, (2) predictive maintenance and condition monitoring to prevent failures, (3) rapid fault detection and repair procedures to minimize MTTR, and (4) modular hot-swappable architecture to enable in-field recovery without full shutdown. Graceful degradation logic preserves partial availability during transient faults.

potential_sources: Redundancy strategy (DP-004.1a) is tightly coupled to control logic and failover timing; must be decoupled from specific subsystem implementations to avoid over-specification.
Maintenance scheduling (DP-004.1b) depends on accurate failure-rate data and operational context; coupling risk if maintenance intervals are not independently tunable per deployment environment.

Fault detection (DP-004.1c) and modular architecture (DP-004.1d) are moderately coupled; isolation mechanisms must be designed to work across multiple module boundaries without creating hidden dependencies.
Graceful degradation (DP-004.1e) introduces coupling between nominal and reduced-capacity operating modes; must ensure mode transitions do not cascade failures or violate safety constraints.
coupling_check.notes: The composite DP is logically sound but requires careful decomposition into independent sub-DPs at the next level to avoid architectural coupling. Redundancy and modularity are complementary; failover logic must be abstracted from subsystem details. Maintenance and diagnostics should be decoupled from operational control to allow independent tuning.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be procured, integrated, and maintained within cost and weight/space budgets; risk: redundancy overhead may exceed acceptable limits for resource-constrained deployments.
Assumption: Failure modes are well-characterized and predictable; risk: unknown failure modes or cascading failures may not be mitigated by the selected DP.
Assumption: Spare parts and repair expertise are available on-site or accessible within acceptable lead times; risk: supply-chain delays or lack of trained personnel could violate MTTR targets.

Assumption: Graceful degradation does not violate functional or safety requirements; risk: reduced-capacity operation may be unsafe or unacceptable for certain worksite conditions (e.g., load-bearing, safety-critical tasks).

Assumption: Modular hot-swap design is feasible without introducing new failure modes (e.g., connector wear, misalignment); risk: modularity may increase complexity and introduce new points of failure.

Assumption: Condition-monitoring sensors and diagnostics are reliable and do not themselves become availability bottlenecks; risk: monitoring infrastructure failure could mask or delay detection of primary faults.

Assumption: Evaluation period is representative of steady-state operation; risk: startup transients, seasonal variations, or worksite-specific stressors may not be captured in the agreed evaluation window.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-004.1 is well-formed and the selected composite DP is appropriate. Recommend decomposing the DP into independent sub-DPs at the next hierarchical level: (1) DP-004.1.1 for redundancy and failover architecture, (2) DP-004.1.2 for maintenance and condition monitoring, (3) DP-004.1.3 for fault detection and repair procedures, (4) DP-004.1.4 for modular hot-swap design, and (5) DP-004.1.5 for graceful degradation logic. Each sub-DP should be mapped to distinct implementation modules (IM) to enable independent ownership, testing, and evolution. Additionally, clarify acceptance criteria: specify whether 0.97 availability is measured as uptime ratio, mean-time-between-failures (MTBF), or a composite metric; define evaluation period length and operational profile (duty cycle, environmental conditions, worksite constraints) to ensure measurability and reproducibility.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level availability architecture encompassing redundancy coordination, predictive maintenance orchestration, fault detection routing, and graceful degradation logic across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services)

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests health metrics, sensor data, and operational state from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to feed predictive maintenance and condition monitoring

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Directs failover commands, maintenance scheduling, and graceful degradation mode transitions to Vehicle and Teleoperation

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives critical fault notifications from Safety Services; broadcasts availability state changes and degradation alerts back to Safety Services

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Coordinates redundancy failover signaling and rapid fault detection across distributed subsystem instances

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the system-level orchestrator responsible for composite availability strategy. Redundancy coordination, predictive maintenance scheduling, fault detection routing, and graceful degradation logic all naturally reside at this level. Minor diffusion: actual failover execution and hot-swap mechanics partially diffuse into Vehicle module, but control logic and decision-making remain centralized.

Weak containment: Vehicle is a subsystem, not the orchestrator. While it implements modular hot-swappable architecture and hosts redundant components, the composite strategy logic (predictive maintenance scheduling, cross-subsystem failover coordination, graceful degradation across Teleoperation and Safety Services) must diffuse to NGHE Solution level. Redundancy and failover for Vehicle-specific subsystems fit here, but not the system-wide strategy.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a subsystem focused on remote control and communication. Predictive maintenance, redundancy coordination across Vehicle and Safety Services, and system-level graceful degradation logic do not belong here. Only Teleoperation-specific failover and condition monitoring would fit; the composite strategy diffuses heavily.

Weak containment: Safety Services handles safety-critical alerts and constraints, not availability orchestration. While it receives and acts on availability state changes, it does not own the composite strategy logic. Predictive maintenance, redundancy failover, and graceful degradation coordination belong at system level, not within Safety Services.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (System Architecture & Availability Management team)
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP-NGHE-004.1 is a system-level availability strategy that inherently requires orchestration across multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). The NGHE Solution module boundary is the only appropriate container because it owns the composite logic, decision-making, and coordination mechanisms. Subsystem modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) implement their respective portions of redundancy and failover, but the strategy itself—predictive maintenance scheduling, cross-subsystem fault detection routing, and graceful degradation mode transitions—must reside at the system level to avoid fragmentation and ensure coherent availability management.
containment_risks: Risk: Diffusion of failover execution logic into Vehicle and Teleoperation modules may create tight coupling if not carefully abstracted through well-defined interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link).
Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms and condition monitoring thresholds may need to be tuned per subsystem, requiring configuration or delegation patterns to avoid monolithic NGHE Solution code.

Risk: Graceful degradation logic must coordinate state across multiple subsystems; if subsystem state machines are not exposed via Telemetry interface, NGHE Solution will lack visibility and containment will degrade.

Risk: Hot-swappable architecture requires Vehicle module to support dynamic component replacement; if Vehicle does not provide clear module boundaries and health reporting, NGHE Solution cannot reliably orchestrate in-field recovery.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. DP-NGHE-004.1 is appropriately scoped as a system-level composite strategy. To strengthen containment and reduce diffusion risk, recommend clarifying the DP with explicit sub-strategies: (1) NGHE Solution owns availability orchestration, predictive maintenance scheduling, and graceful degradation logic; (2) Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services each own their subsystem-specific redundancy, failover execution, and condition monitoring, exposed via Telemetry and Tasking interfaces; (3) SafetyAlert and Network Link serve as the coordination channels. This layered approach keeps the composite strategy logic centralized while distributing implementation responsibility appropriately.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-004.1 directly addresses FR-NGHE-004.1 by specifying a composite availability strategy (redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid fault detection, modular hot-swappable architecture, graceful degradation) that logically maps to achieving the target availability threshold (≥0.97). The DP is solution-agnostic and does not prescribe implementation details prematurely. All four pillars of the DP are necessary and sufficient to support the functional requirement.

diffusion_risks: Failover execution logic is partially diffused into Vehicle and Teleoperation modules; risk of tight coupling if Tasking interface is not rigorously abstracted and versioned. Recommend explicit failover state machine contracts in interface specifications.

Predictive maintenance algorithms and condition monitoring thresholds require per-subsystem tuning; risk of monolithic NGHE Solution code if configuration/delegation patterns are not established. Recommend parameterized maintenance policies exposed via Telemetry interface.

Graceful degradation logic depends on real-time subsystem state visibility; if Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services do not expose complete state machines via Telemetry, NGHE Solution will lack observability and containment will degrade. Recommend mandatory state reporting schema in Telemetry interface contract.

Hot-swappable architecture requires Vehicle module to support dynamic component replacement and health reporting; if Vehicle does not provide clear module boundaries and rapid health status updates, NGHE Solution cannot reliably orchestrate in-field recovery. Recommend explicit component lifecycle management interface in Vehicle module.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score as the system-level orchestrator. The composite strategy logic (predictive maintenance scheduling, cross-subsystem failover coordination, graceful degradation mode transitions) is appropriately centralized. Minor diffusion (0.08) into Vehicle and Teleoperation is acceptable and expected for failover execution and subsystem-specific redundancy implementation. Subsystem modules (Vehicle: 0.45, Teleoperation: 0.35, Safety Services: 0.40) correctly exhibit lower containment scores because they implement portions of the strategy, not the strategy itself. The IM boundary is well-chosen; no alternative module would provide better containment without creating fragmentation or circular dependencies.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability measurement is directly verifiable against the acceptance criterion (≥0.97 over agreed evaluation period). Telemetry interface provides continuous health metrics and operational state ingestion; Tasking interface enables command traceability for failover and maintenance actions; SafetyAlert and Network Link interfaces support fault detection and state synchronization logging. Recommend instrumenting NGHE Solution with availability counters (uptime/downtime per subsystem, failover event counts, MTTR measurements) and exposing via monitoring dashboard. Graceful degradation state transitions should be logged and correlated with Telemetry data to validate that partial availability is preserved during transient faults.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns availability orchestration and composite strategy logic; Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own subsystem-specific redundancy, failover execution, and condition monitoring. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) define responsibility boundaries. However, recommend explicit allocation matrix documenting which team owns each sub-strategy: (1) predictive maintenance scheduling (NGHE Solution), (2) Vehicle redundancy and hot-swap mechanics (Vehicle team), (3) Teleoperation failover and link redundancy (Teleoperation team), (4) Safety Services alert routing and degradation constraints (Safety Services team). This will prevent ownership ambiguity during implementation.

recommended_revisions: Clarify DP-NGHE-004.1 with explicit sub-strategy ownership: (1) NGHE Solution orchestrates predictive maintenance scheduling, cross-subsystem failover coordination, and graceful degradation logic; (2) Vehicle implements modular hot-swappable architecture and redundant component failover; (3) Teleoperation implements link redundancy and failover; (4) Safety Services enforces availability constraints and receives degradation alerts.

Strengthen Telemetry interface contract: mandate subsystem state machine schema (operational, degraded, failed, recovering) with health metric timestamps and component-level status. This ensures NGHE Solution has complete visibility for graceful degradation decisions.

Strengthen Tasking interface contract: define failover command semantics (target subsystem, failover mode, rollback procedure) and execution acknowledgment protocol. This prevents ambiguity in failover execution and enables MTTR measurement.

Establish predictive maintenance configuration pattern: expose maintenance policy parameters (condition thresholds, scheduling windows, component lifecycle limits) as NGHE Solution configuration, not hardcoded logic. This enables per-subsystem tuning without code duplication.

Add explicit hot-swappable component lifecycle interface to Vehicle module: define component health reporting, replacement readiness checks, and in-field swap procedures. This enables NGHE Solution to orchestrate rapid in-field recovery without full shutdown.

Instrument NGHE Solution with availability metrics: uptime/downtime counters per subsystem, failover event counts, MTTR measurements, graceful degradation state transitions. Log all events with timestamps for post-evaluation analysis against the ≥0.97 acceptance criterion.

Document worksite safety plan integration: clarify how graceful degradation modes interact with safety constraints (e.g., can Teleoperation operate in degraded mode without full Vehicle redundancy?). Recommend SafetyAlert interface carry safety-aware degradation constraints.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass, confirming that FR-NGHE-004.1 is well-formed and DP-NGHE-004.1 is logically sound with acceptable architectural packaging. However, the gate results expose critical diffusion risks and ownership ambiguities that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. The recommended revisions are not cosmetic; they address safety-critical failover semantics, interface contracts, and allocation clarity that directly impact verification and implementation. Given high safety criticality, proceeding to sub-FRs without resolving these revisions risks propagating ambiguity into lower-level requirements and creating verification gaps. Revise DP-NGHE-004.1 and its interface specifications to incorporate the six recommended revisions, then re-gate before decomposing.

revise.why: DP-NGHE-004.1 must be clarified to explicitly partition sub-strategy ownership across NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. The current DP is solution-agnostic (correct for the gate), but lacks the specificity needed to guide interface contracts and prevent diffusion during implementation. Revise DP-NGHE-004.1 to state: 'Achieve ≥0.97 availability through: (1) NGHE Solution orchestrates predictive maintenance scheduling, cross-subsystem failover coordination, and graceful degradation mode transitions; (2) Vehicle implements modular hot-swappable architecture with component-level redundancy and rapid health reporting; (3) Teleoperation implements link redundancy and failover with state machine contracts; (4) Safety Services enforces availability constraints and receives degradation alerts via SafetyAlert interface.' This clarification does not change the DP logic but makes ownership and interface responsibilities explicit, enabling rigorous IM-level packaging and verification.

assumptions: Subsystem modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) will implement their allocated portions of the composite availability strategy without creating circular dependencies or tight coupling.

Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces will be strengthened with explicit state machine schemas, failover command semantics, and health metric contracts before implementation begins.
Predictive maintenance policies and hot-swappable component lifecycle management can be parameterized and exposed via configuration interfaces without requiring code duplication across subsystems.
NGHE Solution will have sufficient real-time visibility into subsystem state machines to make graceful degradation decisions without introducing unacceptable latency or observability gaps.

Safety constraints (worksite safety plan, degradation mode limits) can be encoded in SafetyAlert interface contracts and enforced by Safety Services without blocking legitimate availability recovery actions.
open_risks: Failover execution logic is partially diffused into Vehicle and Teleoperation modules; if Tasking interface contracts are not rigorously versioned and tested, failover semantics may diverge between NGHE Solution orchestration and subsystem execution, causing MTTR to exceed acceptable thresholds.

Predictive maintenance algorithms require per-subsystem tuning; if configuration/delegation patterns are not established, NGHE Solution code may become monolithic and difficult to maintain, increasing risk of logic errors during maintenance scheduling.

Graceful degradation logic depends on complete subsystem state visibility; if Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services do not expose mandatory state machine schemas via Telemetry, NGHE Solution will lack observability and containment will degrade, potentially violating the ≥0.97 availability target.

Hot-swappable architecture requires Vehicle module to support dynamic component replacement; if Vehicle does not provide clear module boundaries and rapid health status updates, NGHE Solution cannot reliably orchestrate in-field recovery, increasing downtime risk.

Safety-aware degradation constraints may conflict with availability recovery actions (e.g., can Teleoperation operate in degraded mode without full Vehicle redundancy?); if SafetyAlert interface does not carry explicit degradation constraints, Safety Services and NGHE Solution may make conflicting decisions during transient faults.

Availability measurement and verification depend on instrumentation (uptime/downtime counters, failover event counts, MTTR measurements); if NGHE Solution is not instrumented with these metrics from the start, post-evaluation analysis against the ≥0.97 criterion may be inconclusive or require costly retrofit.
evidence_needed: Revised DP-NGHE-004.1 with explicit sub-strategy ownership and interface responsibility allocation.
Strengthened Telemetry interface contract specifying mandatory subsystem state machine schema (operational, degraded, failed, recovering) with health metric timestamps and component-level status.
Strengthened Tasking interface contract defining failover command semantics (target subsystem, failover mode, rollback procedure) and execution acknowledgment protocol.

Predictive maintenance configuration pattern specification: parameterized maintenance policies (condition thresholds, scheduling windows, component lifecycle limits) exposed via NGHE Solution configuration, not hardcoded.
Explicit hot-swappable component lifecycle interface specification for Vehicle module: component health reporting, replacement readiness checks, in-field swap procedures.

Instrumentation specification for NGHE Solution: availability metrics (uptime/downtime per subsystem, failover event counts, MTTR measurements, graceful degradation state transitions) with logging and monitoring dashboard exposure.

Worksite safety plan integration document: clarification of how graceful degradation modes interact with safety constraints, with explicit SafetyAlert interface constraints for degradation-aware safety decisions.

Allocation matrix documenting team ownership: (1) predictive maintenance scheduling (NGHE Solution team), (2) Vehicle redundancy and hot-swap mechanics (Vehicle team), (3) Teleoperation failover and link redundancy (Teleoperation team), (4) Safety Services alert routing and degradation constraints (Safety Services team).
Re-gate results (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) after revisions to confirm that diffusion risks are mitigated and ownership ambiguities are resolved.
